questions.q: The Koh-i-Noor is one of the world's most famous examples of what?
questions.explanation: The Koh-i-Noor is one of the most famous diamonds in the world.

questions.q: What does the name 'Koh-i-Noor' mean?
questions.explanation: Koh-i-Noor is Persian for 'Mountain of Light'.

questions.q: To whom was the Koh-i-Noor surrendered after the annexation of Punjab in 1849?
questions.explanation: The diamond was surrendered to the British Crown following the annexation of the Punjab in 1849.

questions.q: The Koh-i-Noor passed through the courts of several dynasties. Which of these famously held it in India before the Persians and Afghans?
questions.explanation: The Mughals held the Koh-i-Noor before it passed to Persian and Afghan rulers.

questions.q: Which Sikh ruler held the Koh-i-Noor before it passed to the British?
questions.explanation: Maharaja Ranjit Singh held the Koh-i-Noor at the Sikh court before British annexation.

questions.q: The Koh-i-Noor today forms part of whose royal collection?
questions.explanation: The Koh-i-Noor is part of the British Crown Jewels held in the Tower of London.

questions.q: The Koh-i-Noor is widely believed to have originally come from mines in which region of India?
questions.explanation: The diamond is traditionally said to have been mined in the Golconda region of southern India, famed for its diamonds.

questions.q: A Persian invader carried the Koh-i-Noor off from Delhi in 1739. Who was he?
questions.explanation: Nadir Shah of Persia carried the Koh-i-Noor off when he sacked Delhi in 1739.

questions.q: Along with the Koh-i-Noor, Nadir Shah also carried off which famous Mughal object in 1739?
questions.explanation: Nadir Shah took both the Koh-i-Noor and the jewelled Peacock Throne when he sacked Delhi.

questions.q: In which country is the Koh-i-Noor diamond kept today?
questions.explanation: The Koh-i-Noor is kept in the United Kingdom as part of the Crown Jewels.

questions.q: It is widely reported that Nadir Shah gave the diamond its name 'Koh-i-Noor' upon seeing it. What does the exclamation translate to?
questions.explanation: Tradition holds that Nadir Shah exclaimed 'Koh-i-Noor!' meaning 'Mountain of Light' on first seeing the diamond.

questions.q: After Nadir Shah's assassination, the Koh-i-Noor passed to which Afghan dynasty's founder?
questions.explanation: Following Nadir Shah's death, the diamond passed to Ahmad Shah Durrani, founder of the Durrani Afghan empire.

questions.q: Ranjit Singh acquired the Koh-i-Noor around 1813 from which deposed Afghan ruler?
questions.explanation: Ranjit Singh obtained the diamond from the exiled Afghan ruler Shah Shuja Durrani around 1813.

questions.q: The treaty by which the Koh-i-Noor was ceded to the British was signed after which event?
questions.explanation: The Koh-i-Noor was ceded under the Treaty of Lahore terms following the British annexation of the Punjab in 1849.

questions.q: Which young Sikh Maharaja was formally made to surrender the Koh-i-Noor to the British?
questions.explanation: The child Maharaja Duleep Singh, last ruler of the Sikh Empire, was made to surrender the Koh-i-Noor in 1849.

questions.q: To which British monarch was the Koh-i-Noor presented in 1850?
questions.explanation: The Koh-i-Noor was presented to Queen Victoria in 1850 after being brought to Britain.

questions.q: The Koh-i-Noor was displayed to the British public in 1851 at what event?
questions.explanation: The diamond was shown to the public at the Great Exhibition of 1851 in London's Crystal Palace.

questions.q: Because its first display underwhelmed viewers, the Koh-i-Noor was later subjected to what process?
questions.explanation: The Koh-i-Noor was re-cut and polished in the 1850s to increase its brilliance, reducing its weight considerably.

questions.q: Several countries have made claims for the return of the Koh-i-Noor. Which of these is among the claimants?
questions.explanation: India, along with Pakistan, Afghanistan and Iran, has made claims for the return of the Koh-i-Noor.

questions.q: The Koh-i-Noor has been set into the crowns of British queen consorts rather than kings, partly due to what reputation?
options: A claim it loses its shine for kings
A tradition reserving it for the eldest child
A legend that it is unlucky for men
questions.explanation: A persistent legend holds that the Koh-i-Noor brings misfortune to male owners, so it has been set in consorts' crowns.

questions.q: Before reaching the Mughals, the Koh-i-Noor is associated in tradition with rulers of which earlier Indian dynasty in the Deccan and beyond?
questions.explanation: Tradition links the diamond to the Kakatiya dynasty of Warangal before its passage to later powers, though early history is uncertain.

questions.q: The Koh-i-Noor is currently set in which item of British regalia?
questions.explanation: The Koh-i-Noor is set at the front of the crown made for Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother.

questions.q: After its 1852 re-cutting, the Koh-i-Noor's weight was reduced to approximately how many carats?
questions.explanation: The re-cut Koh-i-Noor weighs approximately 105.6 carats, down from around 186 carats before.

questions.q: The Koh-i-Noor was brought to England in 1850 aboard a ship of which company?
questions.explanation: The East India Company arranged for the diamond's transport to Britain aboard HMS Medea in 1850.

questions.q: The 1852 re-cutting of the Koh-i-Noor was carried out under the supervision of which firm of jewellers/lapidaries?
options: Garrard, with Dutch cutters from Coster
questions.explanation: The Crown jewellers Garrard oversaw the re-cutting, with cutters from the Coster firm of Amsterdam doing the work.

questions.q: Some accounts link the Koh-i-Noor with the legendary Syamantaka gem and with which epic-era figure?
questions.explanation: Folklore associates the diamond with the Syamantaka jewel of Hindu legend connected to Krishna, though this is legendary rather than documented history.

questions.q: Under the terms of which treaty document was the Koh-i-Noor's cession to Queen Victoria specifically written in?
questions.explanation: The 1849 Treaty of Lahore terms required that the Koh-i-Noor be surrendered to the Queen of England.

questions.q: The Mughal emperor most often associated with possessing the diamond in his Peacock Throne era was which ruler?
questions.explanation: The diamond is traditionally linked to Shah Jahan's era and his jewelled Peacock Throne, though precise identification is debated.

questions.q: When first cut and displayed, the Koh-i-Noor had what shape that British viewers found unimpressive?
options: An old Mughal-cut, rounded dome form
questions.explanation: In its original Mughal cut the stone was a high, rounded dome that lacked the sparkle of modern brilliant cuts, prompting re-cutting.

questions.q: The earliest reasonably reliable written records of the diamond that became the Koh-i-Noor place it with which dynasty's founder's memoir?
questions.explanation: Babur's memoir, the Baburnama, mentions a famous great diamond often identified with the Koh-i-Noor, among the earliest plausible records.
